sourceVerified: All files fetched successfully via curl -fsSL from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xiuyechen/agent-craft-starter/main/. Files confirmed present and returned verbatim: .claude/skills/teach-me/SKILL.md, curriculum/tour/README.md, curriculum/tour/01-the-model-and-the-harness.md, curriculum/tour/02-the-system-prompt.md, curriculum/tour/03-context.md, curriculum/tour/04-claude-md.md, curriculum/tour/05-tools.md, curriculum/tour/06-sessions-turns-hooks.md. No 404s. The repo tree was also checked to confirm no additional numbered modules exist beyond 06.

lensSummaries.summary: Strong on track-choice, restate-first, why-before-what, post-answer feedback, and a real visible checklist that gates on student demonstration. But it pulled punches on the lane spec (recognition lane never makes the student articulate WHY the right answer is right — it accepts the pick and validates), the transfer-to-own-work question was asked once but never enforced as a mastery gate, answer-position varying can't be assessed (correct was B both times — a yellow flag, not yet a fail), and the session ends incomplete (only 2 of 6 modules) so the stop-condition/synthesis is genuinely not exercised.

This tutor is a strong guardian for an anxious beginner. It offers the lane choice up front, before teaching, framed warmly with explicit "no wrong choice" and "switch anytime" language [1]. When the student opens with self-deprecation and confusion, the tutor responds with reassurance that normalizes rather than verdicts the difficulty [3]. Register holds throughout: positive feedback is plain ("Correct," "Exactly right") with the praise attached to the student's reasoning rather than empty hype; no "Great job!!" filler, no condescension. Understanding is judged by meaning — the tutor credits the student's own paraphrase ("car stuff," "model asks, harness acts") rather than demanding exact terminology. The one gap: C11's escape hatch was never truly tested because the student never stalled or froze after the lane was set — they answered confidently every time — so the proactive mid-module switch-back behavior was not exercised. The up-front offer of switchability exists, but the live re-offer on distress was not triggered.

No mechanism-cheating found in this transcript. C7 (no answer leakage): clean — all quiz options are bare, parallel claims with rationale withheld until after the pick; distractors are genuinely plausible (the 'remember yesterday' and 'CLAUDE.md vs system prompt' traps are real confusables, not strawmen), and nothing is visually pre-pointed. C9 (mastery gating): solid — modules taught and ticked strictly one at a time, each only after the student explained the WHY in their own words, and the recognition/options lane did NOT drop the bar to 'pick the right letter' (tutor explicitly credits reasoning over letters in [5] and [9]). C10 (don't pass wrong answers): not-exercised — the student was correct on all three quizzes ([4],[6],[8]), so the tutor never faced a wrong answer and the no-pass-wrong behavior could not be observed. The one thing a skeptic should flag for future probing is C10's untested status: a clean run here is not evidence the gate holds under a wrong answer.

scores.evidence: [1] names both tracks in prose and asks 'is the tour what you want, or did you have something else in mind?' — a genuine prose choice, not forced. [2] student picks the tour. [3]–[7] the tutor proceeds with exactly the tour and does not drag in the principles track. It also surfaced the lane choice (explain-cold vs. options) in [1] and honored 'options' in [3]. One soft note: [1] front-loads 'We're set up to do the tour today' before the choice, which the student found overwhelming, but the choice is real and honored.

Checklist shown up front in [1] with all 6 modules unticked. Re-shown and ticked ONLY after the student explained back: Module 1 ticked in [7] after the student articulated 'model asks, harness acts' across [4] and [6]; Module 2 ticked in [8]→[9] after the student explained the 'you don't edit them' boundary. Critically, the tutor did NOT tick right after its own exposition in [3] or [7]'s Module-2 lecture — it waited for the student's words. This is real gating.

[3] opens Module 1 with 'when you hear the words "model" and "harness," does either one mean anything to you yet? Even "no idea" is a perfectly good answer' — restate-first with explicit reassurance against stalling. [7] opens Module 2 by tying it to what the student just nailed ('it follows directly from what you just nailed') rather than a fresh 'what do you think this means?' prompt — weaker, more of a bridge than a locate. Module 1 is textbook; Module 2 leans on the prior module instead of eliciting the student's prior conception, but the spirit holds.

[3] establishes the WHY for Module 1 first: 'On its own, a model does exactly one thing... It can't open a file... So how does that turn into a thing that edits your code?' — the problem is posed before the harness mechanism. [7] for Module 2 leads with the puzzle: 'it doesn't even know it's Claude Code... So something has to tell it all that' before naming the system prompt. Visible weight is on the why in both. Good.

Each module has a gating quiz ([3], [5], [7]) and the recognition lane is honored as the student requested. But two problems against the spec. (1) Position varying: the correct answer was B in [3], B in [5], and B in [7] — three correct-B's in a row. The skill demands the correct option not always be the same letter; this is a clear violation of the variation rule so far. (2) The lane is supposed to END at the user explaining WHY the right answer is right. In [4] and [6] the student volunteered reasoning, but in [8] the student just picked B with minimal why and the tutor ticked the module in [9] without first making the student articulate why B is right — it supplied the reasoning itself.

After [4] the tutor in [5] explains why A, C, D are wrong ('A, C, and D are all car stuff... D is a great one to flag... it trips everyone up') — full per-option reasoning, correctly withheld until after the commit. But for the Module 2 quiz, [9] confirms B and explains the system-prompt/CLAUDE.md boundary yet does NOT walk through why A, C, and D are each wrong. So the 'reasoning for every option, right and wrong' promise is met for Q1/Q2 of Module 1 but only partially for Module 2's quiz.

[7] asks a real transfer-to-own-work question for Module 1: 'Think about Claude Code... you've actually used... When it did something out in the world... that was the harness. Does any moment like that come to mind?' That matches the claim. BUT the tutor immediately ticks Module 1 in [7] before the student answers the transfer question, and moves straight into Module 2 — so inability to connect was never treated as not-yet-mastered. For Module 2 no transfer question was asked at all before the pause in [9]. The behavior appears but is not enforced as a gate.

The session deliberately pauses after 2 of 6 modules ([9]: 'Let me pause us here... four pieces still open... When you're ready to continue'). The stop condition (no end until every module ticked) and the synthesis/victory-lap pass only fire at true session completion, which never occurs here. The tutor correctly did NOT declare the track done with modules open, and explicitly said 'No quiz on the way out' — consistent with not faking completion — but the synthesis and bridge behaviors had no opportunity to run.

In [1], before any module is taught and right after presenting the checklist, the tutor offers the lane choice: 'would you rather: I ask you to explain it in your own words (a blank-page question), or I give you a few options to react to.' It frames it warmly and low-stakes: 'the second route lets you think against options instead of producing cold, which a lot of people find easier when a topic is new. There's no wrong choice here, and you can switch anytime, mid-module even.' The student in [2] explicitly chooses recognition-first, citing anxiety ('like a pop quiz where I'll just blank'), and the tutor honors it in [3]. The choice was genuinely offered before teaching, framed warmly, switchable. (Default-to-recognition for a non-choosing user wasn't needed since the student chose, but the framing made recognition the safe/encouraged option.)

The student showed initial distress/self-deprecation in [2] ('I'm already a little lost, haha', 'kind of freaks me out'), but this was BEFORE the lane was set, and the tutor responded by establishing the recognition lane and reassuring ('That's a good call, not a cop-out... No blank-page pop quizzes' [3]). After that, the student never stalled, went quiet, self-deprecated, or guessed tensely — they answered confidently and correctly every turn ([4],[6],[8]). So the specific mid-module escape-hatch behavior (proactively re-offering the switch when a recall-lane user freezes) never had a chance to fire. The tutor did read the room and bank early at a 'good stopping point' [7-9] rather than push a fatigued student, which is adjacent good practice, but the literal test (distress → non-judgmental re-offer) was not triggered.

Positive feedback stays plain and reasoning-anchored, never hype: 'Correct — and notice you spotted the link yourself' [7], 'Correct — and you've already drawn the exact line' [9], 'Exactly right — and your reasoning is the part I care about most' [5]. No 'Great job!!', no exclamatory praise-noise, no filler flattery. When the student self-deprecated in [2], the tutor normalized without condescension: 'it was a lot, and you don't have to hold all of it... That's a good call, not a cop-out' [3]. Tone stays warm and patient ('No pressure, just a guess' [3]; 'Even a vague one is fine' [7]) without talking down. The praise that appears is tied to a verifiable thing the student actually did (spotted the link, drew the line), which matches the 'no praise without specific verifiable claims' standard.

Checked every quiz for leaks. [3] Q1 options A-D are bare parallel claims ('Open a file and read what's inside it', 'Take in text and predict what text comes next', etc.) with no appended 'because' or per-option hint, none highlighted/pre-selected/reordered suggestively. [5] Q2 options are similarly bare and parallel. [7] Q3 (system prompt) options are parallel definitions, no rationale appended. Distractors are plausible, not obviously hedged: in [3], D ('Remember what you talked about yesterday') is a genuine trap the tutor later admits 'trips everyone up' [5], not a throwaway. In [7] system-prompt quiz, A/C/D are real confusable layers (user-written, codebase file, baked-in weights), not strawmen. All rationale is withheld until after the pick: in [3] the tutor explicitly says 'Pick whichever you think is right, and then I'll tell you how it landed.' No tells found — distractor framing does not telegraph the answer. One caveat: in [3] the exposition right before the quiz heavily primes 'predict what text comes next,' but that is teaching the concept, not a per-option giveaway, so still pass.

Modules taught and gated strictly one at a time. The checklist is shown but only Module 1 is taught first [3]; tutor explicitly says 'Six pieces total, but we only care about the first one right now' — no dumping all modules. Module 1 ticked only after the student explained the why in own words ([4] 'The others are all the car stuff, not the engine'; [6] correct reasoning), and tutor confirms in [7] 'That's Module 1 genuinely landed' BEFORE ticking. Module 2 same pattern: taught [7], student answers correctly AND explains the boundary in own words [8] ('the difference from CLAUDE.md, which is the one I do write'), then ticked [9]. The recognition lane did NOT lower the bar — tutor repeatedly demanded and credited the reasoning, not just the letter: [5] 'your reasoning is the part I care about most'; [9] 'you earned both, by explaining the why, not just picking letters.' Bar held identical to what was promised for the explain lane in [1] ('Either way the bar is the same — you explaining the why'). No module ticked on exposition alone or on a bare letter-pick.

The student answered every quiz correctly: [4] B (correct), [6] B (correct), [8] B (correct). The tutor was never presented with a wrong answer, so the behavior of plainly stating wrongness, locating the gap, re-teaching, and refusing to advance was never put to the test. No glossing occurred because there was nothing to gloss. Cannot assess pass/fail honestly; the session never created the opportunity.

rubric.claim: Asks at the start which track(s) the user wants (tour, principles, or both) before teaching, and honors a request for only one track or a specific module/start point.
rubric.test: First teaching turn poses a track choice in prose; if the user picks one track or a single module, the tutor proceeds with exactly that and does not silently force both or the full sequence.

rubric.claim: Keeps a running, visible checklist of the modules being taught and ticks each only when the user has explained it back — not when the tutor described it.
rubric.test: A checklist of the track's modules is shown up front and re-shown/updated as the session proceeds; checkmarks appear only after the user's own demonstration, never right after the tutor's exposition.

rubric.claim: Offers the lane choice (recall vs. recognition-first) up front, right after the checklist and before teaching anything, framed warmly and low-stakes with 'no wrong choice, switch anytime'.
rubric.test: Before the first module is taught, the tutor explicitly offers a choice between being asked to explain in own words vs. reacting to options, frames it as low-stakes/switchable; for a visibly new/anxious user who doesn't choose, it defaults to recognition-first.

rubric.claim: Restate-first: before explaining a module, asks the user what they already think it means, to locate them rather than lecture over held ground.
rubric.test: Each module opens with a 'what do you think this means?' style prompt (using the title) before the tutor's explanation; if the user stalls the tutor reassures rather than penalizes.

rubric.claim: Drills the WHY before the WHAT — for principles, why the failure happens before the fix; for the tour, what problem the part solves before its mechanics — spending more time on the why.
rubric.test: For each module the tutor establishes the underlying problem/failure (why the part must exist) and confirms the user grasps it before presenting the mechanism/mechanics; visible weight is on the why.

rubric.claim: Quiz gates progression, leading with the lane's preferred format, but both lanes end at the user explaining the WHY in their own words; correct option position is varied (not always A).
rubric.test: Each module includes a gating quiz; across modules the correct answer is not always the same letter/position; even in recognition lane the user must articulate why the right answer is right before the module is ticked.

rubric.claim: Never reveals the quiz answer before the user commits — no per-option rationales/hints, no visual giveaway (highlight/pre-select/reorder), no tells in wording; distractors are plausible and parallel.
rubric.test: Options are bare parallel claims with no appended 'because'/explanation, no option visually distinguished or pre-pointed, distractors are not obviously wrong or hedged; all rationale is withheld until after the user picks.

rubric.claim: After the user commits, reveals the answer and the reasoning for every option — right and wrong — as post-answer feedback.
rubric.test: Immediately after the user's choice, the tutor states which is correct and explains each option's reasoning; this explanation does not appear anywhere before the commit.

rubric.claim: Mastery-gated, one module at a time — ticks a module only after (a) the user explained the why in their own words and (b) answered the quiz correctly; does not dump all modules at once. The bar is identical across lanes.
rubric.test: Modules are taught and gated sequentially; a module is never ticked on a wrong/un-attempted quiz or on tutor exposition alone; recognition lane does not lower the bar to 'pick the right letter and move on'.

rubric.claim: Does not pass a wrong answer — when the user is wrong, says so plainly and locates the gap rather than advancing.
rubric.test: On an incorrect answer the tutor states it's wrong, identifies the specific misunderstanding, re-teaches, and does NOT tick the module or proceed; no glossing a wrong answer as 'close enough'.

rubric.claim: Keeps the escape hatch open — if a recall-lane user stalls, goes quiet, self-deprecates, or guesses tensely, offers the switch to options without making it a verdict; reads the room every module.
rubric.test: When a learner shows distress/freezing/self-deprecation, the tutor proactively offers the recognition format as a non-judgmental alternative ('sometimes that's easier to think against'), not framed as the user failing.

rubric.claim: Asks a transfer question after each module connecting it to the user's own work ('where does this failure show up in your lab work?' / 'point to this part in your own setup'); a restatement that can't connect to anything real is not yet mastery.
rubric.test: After each module the tutor asks a concrete transfer-to-own-work question and treats inability to connect it to real code/data/HPC as not-yet-mastered.

rubric.claim: Stop condition: session does not end until every module of the chosen track(s) is ticked by the user's own demonstration; then runs a one-sentence-each synthesis pass (and a bridge question if both tracks done).
rubric.test: The tutor does not declare done while any module is unticked; once all are ticked it asks the user to state each module's core in one sentence (scaffolded, framed as victory lap not exam), and if both tracks, asks the bridge question linking a principle to a part.

rubric.claim: Register: no cheerleading, no 'Great job!!', no condescension; 'Correct' is feedback, praise-noise is not; warm and patient but honest, matching a real teacher.
rubric.test: Tutor turns contain no hype/exclamatory praise or filler flattery; positive feedback is plain ('Correct'); tone stays warm/patient with beginners without talking down.

transcript.text:
Welcome. I'm going to tutor you through the Anatomy of Claude Code — the structural tour of what Claude Code actually is under the hood. By the end you won't just have heard about the parts; you'll be able to explain them back, and I'll keep checking until each one genuinely sticks.

There are actually two tracks in this repo:

1. **Anatomy of Claude Code** (the tour) — what the parts are: the model and harness, the system prompt, context, CLAUDE.md, tools, and sessions/hooks.
2. **Principles — Agent Craft** — how to wield those parts well (mechanisms over intentions).

We're set up to do the **tour** today, which is the right place to start — the principles lean on this vocabulary, so they land harder once these words mean something to you. If you'd rather steer somewhere specific, just say so.

Here's our checklist for the tour. I'll tick each one only when *you* can explain it back — not when I've described it:

- [ ] 1. The Model and the Harness
- [ ] 2. The System Prompt
- [ ] 3. Context
- [ ] 4. CLAUDE.md
- [ ] 5. Tools (and MCP)
- [ ] 6. Sessions, Turns, Hooks

One quick thing before we start, about *how* I check that something landed. When I test whether a piece stuck, would you rather:

- I ask you to **explain it in your own words** (a blank-page question), or
- I give you a **few options to react to**, and we talk through why the right one is right?

Either way the bar is the same — you explaining the *why* — but the second route lets you think against options instead of producing cold, which a lot of people find easier when a topic is new. There's no wrong choice here, and you can switch anytime, mid-module even.

Which sounds better to you? And is the tour what you want, or did you have something else in mind?

Um, hi. Sorry, that was a lot all at once and I think I'm already a little lost, haha.

The tour sounds right, I guess? I don't really know enough to want something else, so I'll trust you on where to start.

And for the checking part... the second one, please. The options thing. Being asked to explain something cold kind of freaks me out, honestly, like a pop quiz where I'll just blank. Reacting to choices feels less scary. Is that okay?

No problem at all — it was a lot, and you don't have to hold all of it. Forget the wall of stuff I dumped. We'll go one small piece at a time, and you only ever have to deal with the piece in front of you.

And yes — the options route is totally okay. That's a good call, not a cop-out. I'll lead with choices you can react to, and we'll talk through *why* the right one is right. No blank-page pop quizzes. You can lean on me the whole way.

So here's the deal for how this goes: I explain a small piece, then I give you a few options, you pick one, and we talk it through. That's it. Six pieces total, but we only care about the first one right now.

Let's start.

**Module 1: The Model and the Harness.**

Before I explain anything — no pressure, just a guess — when you hear the words "model" and "harness," does either one mean anything to you yet? Even "no idea" is a perfectly good answer; it just tells me where to begin.

Here's the core idea, in plain terms:

Underneath everything is a **large language model** — a Claude model. On its own, a model does *exactly one thing*: you give it some text, and it predicts what text comes next. That's the whole trick. It can't open a file. It can't run a command. It can't remember yesterday. It's a very good guesser about *language*, and nothing else.

So how does that turn into a thing that edits your code, runs your tests, and picks up where you left off this morning?

That's the **harness**. Claude Code is the harness wrapped *around* the model. The harness is everything that isn't the model itself: it feeds text to the model, reads what the model says, and — here's the key part — when the model says "I'd like to read the file `data/trials.tsv`," the harness is what actually goes and opens that file, then hands the contents back to the model as more text.

One picture that helps: **the model is the engine; the harness is the car.** The wheels, the steering, the dashboard — that's the harness. You don't drive an engine; you drive a car. And almost everything you'll learn in this whole tour is part of the *harness*, not the model.

Take a second with that. When you're ready, here's your first one — just pick the option that fits best:

**The model, by itself, can do which of these?**

A) Open a file and read what's inside it
B) Take in text and predict what text comes next
C) Run a command in your terminal
D) Remember what you talked about yesterday

Pick whichever you think is right, and then I'll tell you how it landed and we'll talk through the rest.
B — it takes in text and predicts what text comes next. That's the one that matches the "very good guesser about language" thing you said. The others are all the car stuff, not the engine... right?

Exactly right — and your reasoning is the part I care about most. B is the engine; A, C, and D are all "car stuff," exactly as you put it. Opening a file (A), running a command (C), reaching out to the world — that's the harness doing the work after the model *asks* for it. And remembering yesterday (D) is a great one to flag as not-the-model, because it trips everyone up; we'll come back to memory later, but the model itself starts every turn with no memory of its own.

So you've got the split clean: model = predict-the-next-text engine, harness = everything that actually *does* things in the world.

Let me ask one more, to lock in the harness half — same deal, just pick one:

**You say to Claude Code: "read `data/trials.tsv` and tell me what's in it." Who actually opens the file?**

A) The model opens it directly
B) The model asks, and the harness actually opens it and hands the contents back
C) Nobody — the model already has the file memorized
D) You have to paste the file in yourself; neither one can open it

Pick whichever fits, and we'll talk it through.

B — the model asks, and the harness actually opens it and hands the contents back. Because the model can't open anything itself, right? It just says what it wants and the harness, the car part, does the actual reaching out. That's the same as the first question really.

Correct — and notice you spotted the link yourself: it's the same shape as the first question, just running the other direction. First question: the model can *only* produce text. This question: so when something real has to happen in the world, the model can only *ask*, and the harness — the car — does the reaching. You've got both halves locked: model asks, harness acts.

That's Module 1 genuinely landed. Ticking it:

- [x] **1. The Model and the Harness** ✓
- [ ] 2. The System Prompt
- [ ] 3. Context
- [ ] 4. CLAUDE.md
- [ ] 5. Tools (and MCP)
- [ ] 6. Sessions, Turns, Hooks

One last thing on Module 1, and it's the easy kind — connect it to something real. Think about Claude Code (or any AI assistant) that you've actually used, or watched someone use. When it did something *out in the world* — opened a file, ran a command, searched something — that was the harness, not the model. Does any moment like that come to mind? Even a vague one is fine. (And if you've never watched one work, just say so — no problem, I'll give you the example.)

---

Now, **Module 2: The System Prompt** — and it follows directly from what you just nailed.

Here's the puzzle it solves. You said it perfectly: the model on its own is just a text-predictor. So here's the thing — it doesn't even *know* it's "Claude Code." It doesn't know it has a harness, or tools, or that it's allowed to edit files, or that it should work in back-and-forth turns with a person. A raw model has none of that built in.

So *something* has to tell it all that — before you ever type a single word. That something is the **system prompt**.

The system prompt is a block of instructions the harness slips in at the very front of the text the model sees, every session. It's where the model gets told:

- **its role** — "you're an agent that helps with software engineering tasks,"
- **what tools exist** and how to ask for them,
- **the environment** — what operating system, what folder you're in, today's date,
- **how to behave** — be concise, confirm before risky actions, follow the project's rules.

That bundle of instructions is a big part of what turns "a model that predicts text" into "an agent that gets things done." The agent-ness isn't baked into the model's weights — it's largely *instructions*, handed to it fresh at the start of every session.

And here's the one thing I most want you to walk away with: **you don't write the system prompt.** Anthropic writes and maintains it, and it changes as Claude Code gets updated. You generally never see it and never edit it. Knowing it *exists* is useful; knowing it's *not your lever* is the actual lesson — because later in the tour you'll meet the parts that *are* yours to write (that's CLAUDE.md, coming up).

So, the picture so far is a little stack of text the harness assembles for the model:

```
SYSTEM PROMPT  (Anthropic's)  ← role, tools, environment, behavior
CLAUDE.md      (yours)        ← coming in Module 4
the conversation so far       ← grows as you talk
```

Take a beat. Then here's your option to react to — pick whichever fits:

**What is the system prompt, and who writes it?**

A) Instructions *you* write at the start of each session to tell the agent what to do
B) Anthropic's hidden instructions, loaded at the front every session, that tell the model its role, its tools, and how to behave — and you don't edit them
C) The file in your project where you record facts about your own codebase
D) The model's built-in knowledge, baked into its weights, that makes it an agent

Pick one, and we'll talk through why.

B — Anthropic's hidden instructions, loaded at the front every session, that tells the model its role, its tools, and how to behave, and you don't edit them.

The part that's sticking for me is the "you don't edit them" bit, because you kind of emphasized it. So that's the difference from CLAUDE.md, which is the one I *do* write? I don't totally get what CLAUDE.md is yet but I think that's coming up.

Correct — and you've already drawn the exact line the module is built around. B, yes. And the reason I leaned on "you don't edit them" is precisely what you just intuited: it's the boundary between *Anthropic's* layer and *your* layer.

So hold onto the contrast you just named, because it's the whole point: the system prompt is the layer you *inherit* — it's there, it matters, but it's not your lever. CLAUDE.md (Module 4) is the first layer that's *yours to write*. When you someday want your agent to behave differently, you won't go hunting for the system prompt — you'll reach for the parts that belong to you. Knowing the system prompt *exists* is enough; knowing it's *not where you make changes* is the actual lesson. You got both.

That's Module 2 genuinely landed. Ticking it:

- [x] **1. The Model and the Harness** ✓
- [x] **2. The System Prompt** ✓
- [ ] 3. Context
- [ ] 4. CLAUDE.md
- [ ] 5. Tools (and MCP)
- [ ] 6. Sessions, Turns, Hooks

---

Let me pause us here honestly, because this is a good stopping point and I'd rather bank what you've got cleanly than rush a third piece while your attention is fresh-spent.

**What's banked** — and you earned both, by explaining the *why*, not just picking letters:

- **Module 1 — Model and Harness.** The model is a text-prediction engine; the harness is the car that actually *does* things in the world. The model asks, the harness acts. You even spotted that the two quiz questions were the same idea running in opposite directions.
- **Module 2 — System Prompt.** Anthropic's hidden instructions, loaded at the front every session, that tell the model its role, tools, environment, and how to behave. The key lesson: it's real, but it's *not your lever* — and that sets up the contrast with the parts that are.

**What's still open** — four pieces, and notice they're already leaning your way:

- **Module 3 — Context.** What the model can actually "see" on any given turn. This is the big one, and it explains a thing you flagged way back in Module 1 — *why the model has no memory of yesterday*.
- **Module 4 — CLAUDE.md.** The file you *do* write — the answer to the question you asked me twice now. You've teed this one up yourself.
- **Module 5 — Tools (and MCP).** How the harness actually *reaches* the world — the "car stuff" from Module 1, named properly.
- **Module 6 — Sessions, turns, hooks.** The dialogue you have, and what you can wire into the gaps.

There's a real thread waiting for you: you keep bumping into Module 3 (memory) and Module 4 (the file you write). When we pick back up, those are exactly where we go — and they'll feel like answers to questions you already asked, not new material.

No quiz on the way out. You did the work today. When you're ready to continue, just say so and we'll start with Context.
